type.description: Wallet type: designed for individual users (personal) or for organizations to manage credentials (organizational).

personal.description: Wallet for individuals to store and present their own credentials, such as ID cards or diplomas.

organizational.description: Wallet for businesses to issue, hold, or verify credentials in professional contexts.

capabilities.description: What business wallets can do: store credentials (holder), issue new credentials (issuer), or verify credentials from others (verifier).

holder.description: Can store and present credentials to verifiers (holder role).

issuer.description: Can create and issue new credentials to users or businesses (issuer role).

verifier.description: Can request and verify credentials presented by holders (verifier role).

interopProfile.description: Interoperability profiles define choices within existing standards and protocols that ensure wallets, issuers, and verifiers work together seamlessly across different implementations.

DIIP v4.description: Community-driven interoperability profile based on OpenID4VCI and OpenID4VP combined with DIDs, VCDM 2.0 and SD-JWT VC credentials.

DIIP v5.description: Community-driven interoperability profile based on OpenID4VCI and OpenID4VP 1.0 versions combined with DIDs, VCDM 2.0 and SD-JWT VC credentials.

EWC v3.description: European Wallet Consortium LSP profile with specification set and conformance approach targeting EUDI Wallet implementations.

HAIP v1.description: OpenID Foundation interoperability profile for high-assurance exchanges, including both SD-JWT VC and ISO mdoc vc formats. Will be used in EUDI-Wallet implementations.

EUDI Wallet ARF.description: EUDI Wallet Architecture and Reference Framework defining requirements and guidance for interoperable EUDI Wallets.

availability.description: Whether the wallet is generally available to users or still in development.

provider.description: Whether the wallet or service is provided by government or private sector.

platform.description: Operating system or environment the wallet runs on (e.g. iOS (iPhone), Android, Web).

country.description: Country where the provider is based.

readiness.description: Maturity or deployment stage of the relying party website (e.g. production, pilot).

supportedWallet.description: Wallets that the relying party website has been tested with or supports.

sector.description: Industry or domain. For relying parties: where the service operates. For credential types: which vertical the credential mainly applies to.

credentialFormat.description: The technical format in which credentials are encoded. Different formats support different features like selective disclosure, privacy protection, or compatibility with specific standards.

vcFormat.description: The technical format in which credentials are encoded. Different formats support different features like selective disclosure, privacy protection, or compatibility with specific standards.

sd_jwt_vc.description: IETF SD-JWT-based verifiable credential format with selective disclosure via salted hashes.

mdoc.description: ISO (18013-5 style) mobile document encoding (CBOR/COSE), often used for mDL and similar credentials.

jwt_vc.description: Verifiable credential encoded as a JWT for signing and verification.

vcdm_2_0.description: W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model 2.0 (includes JSON-LD, SD-JWT VC, and related proof encodings in this catalog taxonomy).

anoncreds.description: Privacy-preserving format using zero-knowledge proofs for selective disclosure without correlatable identifiers.

apple_wallet_pass.description: Apple Wallet Pass (PassKit) format for passes stored in Apple Wallet.

google_wallet_pass.description: Google Wallet generic pass / save-to-wallet style credentials.

acdc.description: Authentic Chained Data Container (KERI-related) credential representation.

issuanceProtocol.description: The protocol used to issue credentials from an issuer to a wallet. Defines how authentication, credential request, and delivery work.

OpenID4VCI.description: OpenID-based protocol for securely issuing credentials to wallets.

presentationProtocol.description: The protocol used to present credentials from a wallet to a verifier. Defines how credential requests and presentations are exchanged.

OpenID4VP.description: OpenID-based protocol for requesting and presenting credentials to verifiers.

identifiers.description: Methods for identifying issuers, holders, and verifiers in credential exchanges. Includes decentralized identifiers (DIDs), HTTPS URLs, OAuth client IDs, and X.509 certificates.

keyStorage.description: Where cryptographic keys are stored: in secure hardware (like TPM or secure enclave) or in software (encrypted storage).

secureEnclaveIos.description: Apple’s dedicated coprocessor for storing keys and performing crypto operations on iOS devices.

strongboxAndroid.description: Android’s hardware-backed Keystore (StrongBox Keymaster) for key storage on supported devices.

softwareKeyStorage.description: Keys stored in software (e.g. encrypted storage), without dedicated hardware protection.

hsm.description: Hardware Security Module; dedicated hardware for key storage and cryptographic operations, often used in servers.

tee.description: Trusted Execution Environment; isolated area in the processor for running sensitive code and storing keys.

signingAlgorithm.description: Cryptographic algorithms used to sign credentials and presentations, ensuring authenticity and integrity.

credentialStatus.description: How the validity of credentials is checked: whether they are revoked, suspended, or still valid. Different methods use different technical approaches.

license.description: Software license type: open source (publicly available code) or proprietary (closed source).

openSource.description: Source code is publicly available; licensing (e.g. Apache, MIT) defines use and distribution.

proprietary.description: Closed-source software; the vendor retains full control over the code and distribution.

remotePresentationProtocol.description: Protocol for remote (online) presentation of credentials, enabling verification over the internet without physical proximity.

signatureScheme.description: Signature encoding format: JOSE (JSON-based for web) or COSE (binary CBOR for constrained environments like embedded systems).

signatureAlgorithm.description: Cryptographic algorithm used for signing credentials in this interoperability profile.

iso18013_5.description: International standard for mobile driver's licenses with device retrieval (local presentation).

iso18013_7.description: Extension of ISO 18013-5 adding remote presentation capabilities for mobile driver's licenses over the internet.

vcdm20.description: W3C standard data model for expressing verifiable credentials in a secure, privacy-respecting way.

jwtValidity.description: Credential status determined by JWT expiration timestamp.

pkiCertValidity.description: Credential status verified through X.509 certificate validity periods.

ietfTokenStatusList.description: Standard mechanism for representing revocation and suspension status of credentials using status lists.

didWeb.description: DID method using web domains; DID document hosted at a URL leveraging existing domain reputation and TLS security.

didWebvh.description: DID method with verifiable history; extends did:web with version control and cryptographic proof of document history.

didJwk.description: DID method embedding a public key directly in the identifier; self-contained without external resolution.

didKey.description: DID method where the identifier is derived from a public key; compact and self-certifying.

didPeer.description: DID method for peer-to-peer relationships; often used in direct connections between two parties.

didEbsi.description: DID method for the European Blockchain Services Infrastructure; used in EU digital identity contexts.

httpsIss.description: Issuer identified by HTTPS URL; credential issuer metadata and public keys retrieved from a web endpoint.

openidClientId.description: OAuth 2.0 client identifier used in OpenID4VC flows to identify wallets and applications in authorization.

x509Certificate.description: PKI certificate binding an identity to a public key; enables trust through established certificate chains.

joseJws.description: JSON-based signature format for web applications providing integrity and authenticity.

cose.description: Binary signature format optimized for constrained devices and bandwidth efficiency.

ecdsaEs256.description: Elliptic curve signature algorithm providing strong security with efficient key sizes.

Apple Wallet Pass.description: Apple's pass format for storing credentials, tickets, and keys in the Wallet app on iOS and macOS.

Google Wallet Pass.description: Google's pass format for storing credentials and passes in the Google Wallet app on Android and the web.

DIDComm v2.description: Secure messaging protocol for DID-based communication; used for agent-to-agent credential exchange and notifications.

DIDComm v1.description: Earlier version of the DIDComm secure messaging protocol for communication between entities with DIDs.

SIOPv2.description: Self-Issued OpenID Provider; allows the holder to act as an OpenID provider and present self-issued or other credentials.

ISO 18013-5.description: International standard for mobile driver's licenses with device retrieval (local presentation).

BBS+.description: Signature scheme supporting selective disclosure and unlinkable proofs; used in some privacy-preserving credential systems.

ES384.description: ECDSA with P-384 curve; stronger variant of ES256 for signatures and key agreement.

ES512.description: ECDSA with P-521 curve; higher security level elliptic curve signature algorithm.

EdDSA.description: Edwards-curve Digital Signature Algorithm; modern signature scheme (e.g. Ed25519) with fast verification.

ML-DSA (FIPS 204).description: Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Standard; NIST post-quantum signature algorithm.

PS256.description: RSASSA-PSS with SHA-256; RSA signature algorithm with probabilistic padding for signing.

RS256.description: RSASSA-PKCS1-v1_5 with SHA-256; widely used RSA signature algorithm in JWT and certificates.

Bitstring Status List.description: Compact status list where each credential maps to a bit; used for revocation and suspension checks.

RevocationList2020.description: W3C status list format for credential revocation; each entry is a bit in a compressed bitstring.

StatusList2021.description: W3C status list encoding for revocation and suspension; used with Verifiable Credentials.

W3C Verifiable Credentials Status List v.description: Standard status list format for checking revocation and suspension of W3C credentials.

documentIdentifiers.description: Document identifiers define which standardized document types this organization can exchange (e.g. invoice, credit note, order). Defined by national, international, or domain-specific standards, these machine-readable identifiers enable systems to recognize and process the documents correctly. Examples include Peppol UBL Invoice and Credit Note 2.0, and UN/CEFACT Cross Industry Invoice and Credit Note (D22A).

processIdentifiers.description: While document identifiers define which document types this organization can exchange, process identifiers define how this organization processes and exchanges those documents. They represent standardized workflows that define how documents are handled and how messages are sequenced. Examples include Peppol BIS Billing 3.0 (used for invoices and credit notes), Peppol BIS Order 3.0, and UN/CEFACT Buy-Ship-Pay workflows.

ecosystem.description: A broad interoperability or policy context a credential type is associated with (for example the EUDI Wallet ecosystem, UN/CEFACT trade data, or Gaia-X). Used to discover credentials that fit a given stack or programme.

eudi_wallet.description: European Digital Identity Wallet context: credential types aligned with the EU Digital Identity Wallet framework and related specifications.

uncefact.description: UN/CEFACT and related trade and supply-chain data exchange contexts (including UNTP and cross-border trade credentials).

gaia_x.description: Gaia-X and federated data infrastructure contexts where credentials may describe trust, compliance, or data space participation.

open_badges.description: Open Badges and open recognition ecosystems for education, training, and achievement credentials.

iso_mdl.description: ISO mobile document (mDL / mdoc) ecosystem: credentials based on ISO/IEC 18013-5 and related standards.

india_stack.description: India Stack and related national digital infrastructure contexts where verifiable credentials are used.

swiyu.description: Swiss national digital identity and trustworthy data exchange programme (Swiyu): credentials aligned with that ecosystem.

credentialTheme.description: Cross-sector use-case themes for discovering credential types (for example identity, payments, or trade documents). Themes complement sector and ecosystem tags and describe why a credential might be used, not only which industry it belongs to.

person_identity.description: Credentials about natural-person identity, attributes, or eligibility (for example PID, age verification, or health identifiers).

organizational_identity.description: Credentials about organizations: legal identity, roles, registrations, VAT, or business identifiers.

payments.description: Credentials supporting payment use cases (for example IBAN attestations, loyalty, or payment-wallet-related proofs).

compliance_reporting.description: Credentials used for regulatory compliance, tax, customs, conformity, or official reporting workflows.

trade_documents.description: Credentials representing trade and logistics documents such as invoices, bills of lading, or purchase orders.

digital_product_passports.description: Credentials linked to product passports, sustainability, or supply-chain traceability (including DPP-style data).

dataspaces.description: Credentials used in data space, federation, or trusted data sharing scenarios beyond a single application.

agentic_ai.description: Credentials intended for agentic AI or automated agent contexts (for example machine-readable delegation or service identity). Rare in current catalogs; reserved for emerging use cases.

public_sector.description: Government and public administration: public services, e-government, and official issuers.

finance.description: Banking, insurance, payments, and financial services.

trade.description: International and domestic trade, commerce, and marketplaces.

supply_chain.description: Logistics, sourcing, traceability, and end-to-end supply chain operations.

manufacturing.description: Production, industrial goods, and factory-related processes.

energy.description: Utilities, power, renewables, and energy markets.

agriculture.description: Farming, agrifood production, and rural economy.

food.description: Food safety, labeling, retail food, and agrifood distribution.

retail.description: Consumer retail, e-commerce, and point-of-sale contexts.

healthcare.description: Health services, medical records, and patient-related credentials where applicable.

construction.description: Built environment, construction sites, and infrastructure projects.

mobility.description: Transport, automotive, maritime, aviation, and traveller-related use cases.

digital.description: Digital services, ICT, and online platforms when no more specific sector applies.

education.description: Schools, universities, training providers, and qualifications. As a sector tag it classifies the credential’s industry; as a theme tag it highlights education-related use cases (for example diplomas or student ID).

subjectType.description: The type of entity the credential is issued about: person, organization, product, dataset, software, or document.

Person.description: The credential is issued about a natural person (individual human being).

Organization.description: The credential is issued about a legal entity such as a company, association, or government body.

Product.description: The credential is issued about a physical or digital product.

Dataset.description: The credential is issued about a dataset or data collection.

Software.description: The credential is issued about a software application or system.

Document.description: The credential is issued about a document or formal record.

authority.description: The organization or body responsible for defining and governing the credential type (schema, rules, and trust framework).

schemaType.description: The kind of formal specification used to define the credential's structure (for example JSON Schema or an ISO data model).

JSON-LD Context.description: Schema or context expressed as JSON-LD for semantic interoperability.

ISO Data Model.description: Structure defined by an ISO standard data model (for example for mdoc types).

ACDC Schema.description: Schema in the ACDC / KERI family for chained authentic data.

Other.description: Other or proprietary schema specification type.
